items.title: Show HN: AgentWatch – Prevent runaway AI agents with runtime budget enforcement
items.why_it_matters: A solo developer shipped AgentWatch, a tool that sits in front of LLM APIs to enforce budgets and runtime policies, preventing unexpected costs and runaway agent loops. This matters because managing API costs and ensuring agent reliability are critical for shipping real AI applications. You could integrate AgentWatch into your current AI agent experiments to control costs and prevent unexpected behavior this week.

items.title: Show HN: Selixes - Self-hosted LLM failover gateway, budget caps and PII
items.why_it_matters: Someone shipped Selixes, a self-hosted LLM gateway that offers atomic budget caps and PII redaction. This changes how you can manage LLM usage, offering more control over costs and data privacy, which are important for any revenue project. You could explore the Selixes repo this week to understand how to implement budget caps and PII redaction for your own API calls.

items.title: Show HN: Caliper – pass@k reliability testing for Claude Code and Codex skills
items.why_it_matters: A developer shipped Caliper, a lightweight tool for testing the reliability of Claude Code and Codex skills by running them multiple times and providing a pass@k score. This matters because AI outputs are non-deterministic, and knowing how often your prompts succeed is crucial for building robust software. You could use Caliper this week to test the reliability of a specific Claude Code prompt you rely on for your current project.

items.title: Show HN: Use-zerostack – delegate any task to a lightweight coding agent
items.why_it_matters: Someone shipped Use-zerostack, a lightweight coding agent designed to delegate tasks. This directly supports your goal of getting better at the 'direction' part of vibe coding by giving you a new tool to practice delegating coding tasks to AI. You could try delegating a small, well-defined coding task from your AI-ingest project to Use-zerostack this week.

items.title: Show HN: Better Graphs – Teach agents to stop making plain Matplotlib slop
items.why_it_matters: A developer shipped 'Better Graphs,' a project that includes Claude Code skills and design motifs to teach agents how to create higher-quality data visualizations. This matters because it provides concrete examples of how to direct AI (specifically Claude Code) to produce better, more professional output. You could integrate the provided Claude Code instructions or design motifs into your own Claude Code interactions this week when generating any data visualizations.

items.title: Show HN: Claudete – Command a legion of Claude Code instances and shells
items.why_it_matters: Someone shipped Claudete, a weekend project designed to manage multiple Claude Code instances and shell sessions more efficiently, ensuring they persist across restarts. This matters because it directly improves your daily workflow with your primary coding tool, making your life easier and more productive. You could download and try Claudete this week to streamline how you manage your Claude Code sessions.

items.title: Show HN: Argus – Capture, replay and QA every Claude Code session your team runs
items.why_it_matters: Someone shipped Argus, a tool to capture, replay, and QA Claude Code sessions. This matters because it provides a way to review and analyze your interactions with Claude Code, which is essential for refining your 'direction' skills and understanding what works well. You could use Argus this week to record and review a few of your Claude Code sessions, looking for ways to improve your prompts.

items.title: Show HN: Drift, write LLM agents in English and transpile to async Python
items.why_it_matters: Someone shipped Drift, a tool that lets you write LLM agents in English and transpiles them to async Python. This matters because it simplifies the process of building agents, allowing you to focus more on the logic and less on the boilerplate code. You could try building a simple agent with Drift this week to automate a small task related to your AI-ingest project.

items.title: Show HN: The CLI for browser agents
items.why_it_matters: Someone shipped a CLI specifically designed for building browser agents. This matters because it provides a direct, command-line interface for automating web interactions with AI, which could be useful for data collection or testing. You could explore using this CLI this week to build a simple browser agent that scrapes a piece of information relevant to your AI-ingest tool.

items.title: Show HN: A REPL for browsers that agents love
items.why_it_matters: Someone shipped a REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) specifically for browser agents. This matters because it gives you an interactive environment to experiment with and debug browser agent commands, making the development process faster and more intuitive. You could use this REPL this week to interactively test commands for a browser agent you're trying to build.

items.title: I used Claude Code to get a second opinion on my MRI
items.why_it_matters: A developer shared how they used Claude Code Opus for a personal project: analyzing an MRI. This matters because it's a real-world example of someone shipping a personal project using your primary AI tool, demonstrating a practical application beyond typical coding tasks. You could think of a small, personal data analysis task you have and try to use Claude Code to get a 'second opinion' or analysis this week.

items.title: Show HN: Bash4LLM+ – A lightweight, dependency-free Bash wrapper for LLM APIs
items.why_it_matters: Someone shipped Bash4LLM+, a single-file Bash wrapper for interacting with LLMs from the terminal using just Bash, curl, and jq. This matters because it provides a quick, lightweight way to test LLM prompts or integrate LLM calls into shell scripts without needing Python or Node. You could use Bash4LLM+ this week to quickly test different Claude API prompts directly from your terminal.

items.title: Lessons from building SQLite with LLMs
items.why_it_matters: This article shares lessons learned from using LLMs like Claude Code and Codex for complex software engineering tasks, specifically building something like SQLite. This matters because it provides insights into the process of effectively directing AI for significant coding projects, directly addressing your goal of improving your 'direction' skills. You could read through the 'lessons' this week and identify one specific technique for directing AI that you can apply to your AI-ingest project.
